responses: I've called Laradise home since 2004, moving from Georgia where my dad was stationed for his military career. I just celebrated my 20th year of marriage to a 6th generation Wyomingite from Wheatland.

I have 20 years of community building experience in Laramie, as an economic developer, a non profit director and public historian. I am a graduate of Leadership Wyoming, co-founder of the Albany County Housing Coalition, Laramie Mural Project, and am the senior member of the House Appropriations Committee. 

I utilize civility, honesty, optimism and empathy as essential leadership skills to serve my constituents, small businesses and hard working families. I enjoy learning new things and building relationships based on shared values. I am passionate about attainable housing, opportunities for young people, public education, access to mental health services and recreating on public lands.

We need to address the outmigration of our youth if we are to build strong communities. 

Young people want good paying jobs, attainable housing, affordable healthcare, access to mental health and maternity services, vibrant downtowns and outdoor amenities. The state should invest in these things and the systems that support them. 

We should establish an "economic infrastructure" trust as a long term funding source and work with the executive branch to get everyone “rowing” in the same direction. More agencies than just the Wyoming Business Council should be addressing this challenge. The legislature should work with the Dept. of Workforce Services, Tourism, State Parks, Dept. of Family Services, Dept. of Health, etc. to strategically leverage the state's resources to retaining our young people.

Wyoming is one of the most expensive states in the nation for health care. To address this, I will continue to support Medicaid expansion, increases to reimbursement rates for maternal and rural EMS services, educational incentive training and recruitment programs. 

Last session, I voted to use our Rural Health Transformation funds to support rural hospitals, for emergency and maternal care, primary prevention services, behavioral health, workforce education, and telehealth. 

I also support expanding Family and Medical Leave and opening the state Employee Group Insurance plan to school districts, small businesses and the self-employed.

Public lands belong to the public. They are NOT to be traded for corporate gain or sold to the highest bidder for private use. We need to ensure our state agencies that provide recreation (State Parks), hunting opportunities (Game & Fish), and conservation (Forest Service, WWNRT) are staffed appropriately with budgets that allow them to manage the land, collaboratively, with federal partners for the people of Wyoming.

Property tax relief should be targeted to those who need it most. I do not support blanket reductions for those able to pay, i.e. second homeowners and the ultra rich. Collected locally, property taxes provide essential community services - roads, water, emergency response, and public education that we all benefit from. 

I am opposed to further reductions in property taxes as this ensures services will decline, sales taxes will go up, and increases the likelihood of a statewide income tax.

The state should facilitate a landscape where hard working Wyomingites can innovate. I support policies that increase economic opportunities, good paying jobs and investments in community infrastructure. The legislature should facilitate, promote and invest in an entrepreneurial ecosystem that supports homegrown innovation while maximizing economies of scale. 

If we want to retain our young people, we need growth on our own terms. A commitment to making Wyoming the best version of itself. The state should support a robust economy built on our shared values, independent entrepreneurial spirit, and natural assets.

Constituents should have full access to the donations accepted by or spent on behalf of a candidate's campaign. From individual gifts to out-of-state corporations, voters have the right to know who is investing in and influencing our political process. 

More frequent reporting, random audits, and a requirement for organizations buying ads for or against candidates to reveal their funding sources, i.e. "dark money", would improve the transparency of our current system.

questions: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?

We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?
pageProps.questionnaireIntro:
The answers below were solicited from candidates via a written questionnaire created by WyoFile reporters and editors in June 2026. Responses are presented exactly as submitted, without fact-checking, wordsmithing or editing for grammar, punctuation or spelling errors. 

Legislative candidates were invited to respond to the questionnaire several times by email and by phone. Out of fairness to the candidates who met the deadline, WyoFile will not add responses after the guide's publication.
pageProps.aboutProject:
WyoFile produced this guide with coding and web development by Thomas Musselman, editing by Tennessee Watson and production assistance by Calla Shosh. Contact Tennessee Watson with questions, corrections or suggestions at <tennessee@wyofile.com>.

WyoFile is a Wyoming 501(c)3 nonprofit, independent, member-supported, public-interest news service reporting on the people, places and policy of Wyoming. WyoFile's [donor base](https://wyofile.com/supporters/) is politically diverse, including some donors who are candidates in this year's election. We maintain separation between sources of revenue and editorial direction, decisions and oversight. Acceptance of financial support does not constitute implied or actual endorsement of donors or their products, services or opinions, and does not confer editorial influence, nor does donor support imply any endorsement of WyoFile's reporting, commentary or editorial position.

This material is available for republication by select media outlets. Contact Collaborations Editor Rebecca Huntington at <rebecca@wyofile.com> to learn how.
